title: The trust-latency gap: why the future of UX is intentionally slower
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-15, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2Fmod1Bs/1/0100019d912120fa-6cbe5da5-864f-4f01-bd08-e99c59b0075a-000000/pzZ3W-HiyKD0kr-27zgB_kRB6eDpaFPN8AsKHwfySK4=452
summary: The trust-latency gap: why the future of UX is intentionally slower (11 minute read) As AI makes products faster, excessive speed can reduce trust—especially in high-stakes situations—because users need time and visible effort to feel confident in decisions. The concept of “strategic friction” argues that designers should intentionally slow down or add transparency to important or irreversible actions, balancing speed with human psychology so experiences feel trustworthy, not just efficient.

title: Codex Hacked a Samsung TV
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-15, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.calif.io%2Fp%2Fcodex-hacked-a-samsung-tv%3Futm_source=tldrinfosec/1/0100019d9141e2cc-5ecca1b0-d134-4394-818c-f62b6dfc24f2-000000/wh_M-uACEBnWn-TTa3pMK88V21ZBCstuCGbAJQ-N__4=452

title: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-15, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fsecurity.googleblog.com%2F2026%2F04%2Fbringing-rust-to-pixel-baseband.html%3Futm_source=tldrinfosec/1/0100019d9141e2cc-5ecca1b0-d134-4394-818c-f62b6dfc24f2-000000/v_2oi_ta9eBvr5sjaXcApAFK2F9MDo9WzFtHrarba48=452
summary: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband (6 minute read) Google is moving the Pixel 10 modem's DNS parser from C/C++ to Rust to cut memory-safety bugs in a high‑risk, remotely reachable component. The team selected the hickory-proto DNS crate, added no_std support, and integrated it via Pigweed and direct rustc builds, solving code size, allocator, panic, and symbol conflicts. comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-16, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fahrefs.com%2Fblog%2Fwhy-chatgpt-cites-pages%2F%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019d95d45242-1162037f-53e8-4edc-bbf3-cea690d5003a-000000/LqlMENx_aaB3hAsITxZC1jJYRpxUQHCBGrld2m_-Id0=452
summary: Why ChatGPT Cites One Page Over Another (Study of 1.4M Prompts) (11 minute read) ChatGPT is an aggressive editor. It favors its general search index, uses semantic similarity to select and cite sources, and treats Reddit like a textbook. The pages that get cited are the ones whose titles and content match the questions ChatGPT is asking behind the scenes, and that surface through the right retrieval channel.

title: Fixing Webflow staging pages getting indexed in Google
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-16, Reading time: 1 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fsam-penny_how-could-one-of-the-biggest-cms-platforms-share-7448230436276805632-3si-%3Futm_source=tldrmarketing/1/0100019d95f930cf-492b0db1-3727-45b9-afe4-b6fb29a9cea3-000000/mNogXTbEVHYCLoIsSniI1lP0k5YxBhW9JDWd0jCOxdg=452
summary: Fixing Webflow staging pages getting indexed in Google (1 minute read) Check whether your Webflow staging domain is indexed using a site search query in Google. If it is, add the domain to Google Search Console and submit a removal request for temporary deindexing. For a long term fix, add a meta robots noindex tag to pages using custom code or Tag Manager. Then turn off Webflow's SEO setting that claims to disable indexing but actually blocks crawling instead.

title: Do You Even Need a Database?
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-16, Reading time: 15 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.dbpro.app%2Fblog%2Fdo-you-even-need-a-database%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d95fb8420-1ab00956-039d-4201-85a4-1153626a69cf-000000/XiqZRnHLRz0UKFU0dqZFxeH6xeofScBNC43nGMTDYcM=452
summary: Do You Even Need a Database? (15 minute read) Simple file-based storage is often highly performant for many applications. title: Parcae: Doing more with fewer parameters using stable looped models
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-16, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.together.ai%2Fblog%2Fparcae%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019d967718ff-c5ffbb91-81c9-4d3a-a33e-cc0a75225640-000000/SCaSXzKM71fC84ZEO9J0nx_xs2AyIEVAdzkIWkU3RtU=452
summary: Parcae: Doing more with fewer parameters using stable looped models (6 minute read) Parcae is one of the first stable architectures for looped language models. It achieves the quality of a Transformer twice the size with clean, predictable training. Parcae increases the recurrence rather than purely scaling data, creating a new medium to scale quality. The name Parcae is a homage to the three Roman fates: Nona, Decima, and Morta.

title: Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy in LLM Agents
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-16, Reading time: 1 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Farxiv.org%2Fabs%2F2604.09443%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019d967718ff-c5ffbb91-81c9-4d3a-a33e-cc0a75225640-000000/zyXI6z39_GWv9YhC4w5KgwTFm1Sr51rAa9hOrRMhIUc=452
summary: Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy in LLM Agents (1 minute read) Researchers propose a Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy (ManyIH) to address instruction conflicts in LLM agents, surpassing traditional models with fixed privilege levels. They introduce ManyIH-Bench, assessing models across 12 privilege levels and 853 agent tasks, finding current models perform poorly at 40% accuracy. This highlights the need for scalable conflict resolution in complex agentic environments.

summary: The Clip Economy (11 minute read) Clips - snippets of long-form content - have taken over the entire media ecosystem. TBPN, a podcast recently purchased by OpenAI for $200 million, only draws in around 7,000 views per episode. However, the average TBPN clip receives around 257,000 views. TBPN bakes ads directly into its clips, which helped it generate $5 million in revenue last year. Clips are now the main product for a lot of media, rather than a byproduct.

title: Codex Hacked a Samsung TV
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-17, Reading time: 13 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.calif.io%2Fp%2Fcodex-hacked-a-samsung-tv%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d9b21d03f-00f4895f-1d1d-4b61-869c-aef88546092e-000000/2EniXVIhNZrk4kylFflD303_I0U_JMHaMP_0ifA0p_Y=452
summary: Codex Hacked a Samsung TV (13 minute read) OpenAI's Codex successfully escalated privileges to gain root access on a Samsung Smart TV. Researchers provided Codex with an initial browser shell foothold and the TV's firmware source, tasking the AI with finding a vulnerability to achieve root. Codex identified a critical flaw in the `ntksys` kernel driver, which allowed it to map arbitrary physical memory into user space and overwrite the browser process's kernel credentials.

title: Building a Grow-Only Counter on a Sequentially Consistent KV Store
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-20, Reading time: 14 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fbrunocalza.me%2Fblog%2F2026%2F04%2F13%2Fbuilding-a-grow-only-counter-on-a-sequentially-consistent-kv-store.html%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019daa5d12a4-a8eff751-0bfa-48ba-a070-4de1c73d542d-000000/EETXa5rqgJXMvFQg2NYVrZHdKGUEXoRzqXCNFtEpyG8=452
summary: Building a Grow-Only Counter on a Sequentially Consistent KV Store (14 minute read) A grow-only counter on a sequentially consistent key-value store can fail because stale reads and concurrent updates may lose increments, even when using compare-and-swap. The article explains CRDT-style counters, consistency models, and why a linearizable store or gossip-based design gives more reliable results.

title: PostgreSQL MVCC, Byte by Byte
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-20, Reading time: 9 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fboringsql.com%2Fposts%2Fpostgresql-mvcc-byte-by-byte%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019daa5d12a4-a8eff751-0bfa-48ba-a070-4de1c73d542d-000000/V0jZ4uTXXPfx3nTelGNmNp-gmYMkDThsYIvRXG8Gi7g=452
summary: PostgreSQL MVCC, Byte by Byte (9 minute read) PostgreSQL's MVCC works by storing multiple versions of each row on disk. Every tuple contains xmin and xmax fields that determine its visibility to transactions. Readers never block writers, and writers never block readers. When a row is updated or deleted, a new version is created instead of overwriting the old one. Old versions are cleaned up later by the vacuum process.

title: Data Shift vs. Data Drift
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-20, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.montecarlodata.com%2Fblog-data-shift-vs-data-drift%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019daa5d12a4-a8eff751-0bfa-48ba-a070-4de1c73d542d-000000/OGFxF8g85gTnhRrHxI4qC8V2y1FXIDzgx-37aVF3_WA=452
summary: Data Shift vs. Data Drift (6 minute read) Data drift refers to gradual changes in the statistical properties of data (e.g., mean, distribution), while data shift is a sudden, more severe change often caused by upstream system changes, schema updates, or business events. Correctly distinguishing between the two is critical because they require different alerting strategies.
